petitioner_background: The petitioner is a citizen of Nigeria who sought immigrant classification under section 204(a)(1)(A)(iii) of the Act as an alien battered or subjected to extreme cruelty by a United States citizen (VAWA self-petition). She married I-O-, then a lawful permanent resident (who became a U.S. citizen on June 14, 2002), on February 27, 2001. She filed Form I-360 on October 4, 2004. This is not an EB-1A or O-1A extraordinary ability case.
outcome_reasoning: The AAO granted the motion to reconsider but affirmed its prior dismissal of the appeal. The petitioner failed to establish that she shared a joint residence with her husband as required by section 204(a)(1)(A)(iii)(II)(dd), because her own prior statements (Form I-360 and September 2004 affidavit) claimed the couple began living together in February 2000, while the marriage certificate dated February 27, 2001 listed a different address in Greenbelt, Maryland for her, and her later attempt to blame the discrepancy on her husband was not credible. The petitioner also failed to establish good faith entry into marriage under section 204(a)(1)(A)(iii)(I)(aa), because her five affidavits and supporting statements lacked probative details about courtship, wedding, shared experiences, and how religious differences (Muslim/Christian) were reconciled; supporting affiants focused on abuse rather than the good-faith nature of the marriage, and a single photograph and INS interview letter were insufficient. The burden of proof under section 291 of the Act was not sustained.
legal_citations: Section 204(a)(1)(A)(iii) of the Immigration and Nationality Act, 8 U.S.C. § 1154(a)(1)(A)(iii)
fraud_or_procedural_issues: The AAO found a significant unexplained discrepancy between the petitioner's own statements (Form I-360 and September 20, 2004 affidavit claiming joint residence beginning February 2000) and the February 27, 2001 marriage certificate listing a different Greenbelt, Maryland address for her. The petitioner's attempt on motion to attribute the discrepancy to her husband's error on his permanent residency petition was found not credible, since the conflicting statements originated from the petitioner herself. No explicit fraud finding was made, but the credibility of her good-faith marriage and joint residence claims was rejected.
